title: Superconductivity Reinforces Charge-Density-Wave Phase Coherence across Cuprates
authors: H. Lee, C. -T. Kuo, M. Fujita, C. -C. Kao, J. -S. Lee
abstract: For decades, superconductivity in high-Tc cuprates has been viewed as a competitor that suppresses charge-density-wave (CDW) order by reducing its amplitude and spatial extent. Here, we show that this picture is incomplete, as superconductivity is accompanied by a systematic enhancement of CDW phase coherence across multiple cuprate families. Using resonant soft x-ray scattering combined with a coherence-sensitive momentum-profile analysis, we uncover a BCS-like growth of phase coherence below Tc, which phenomenologically manifests as the absence of CDW peak broadening and near-perfect wave-vector locking. This enhancement remains visible even in a disorder-dominated regime created by long-term crystal aging and follows a common trend when compared with published data on Bi-, Hg-, Y-, and Nd-based cuprates. These results indicate that superconductivity reshapes CDW order in two distinct ways, suppressing its amplitude while strengthening its phase coherence, and reveal an additional phase-level interplay with lattice coupling in high-Tc cuprates.
summary: This paper challenges the long-held view that superconductivity and charge-density-waves are purely competitive in cuprates. It demonstrates that while superconductivity reduces the strength of the CDW, it simultaneously enhances its phase coherence. This suggests a more complex, intertwined relationship between these two fundamental electronic orders.
main_problem: The study investigates whether superconductivity in high-Tc cuprates only suppresses charge-density-wave (CDW) amplitude or if it also influences the CDW phase coherence.
main_result: Superconductivity reinforces CDW phase coherence by narrowing the CDW peak width and stabilizing the wavevector, even while suppressing the CDW amplitude.
method: High-statistics Resonant Soft X-ray Scattering (RSXS) at the Cu L3-edge and O K-edge, combined with a coherence-sensitive momentum-profile analysis and a BCS-like growth model.
model_or_system: The primary system is a La-based cuprate (LSCO) with x=0.115, compared against Bi-, Hg-, Y-, and Nd-based cuprates. The study also utilizes an aged LSCO crystal to explore a disorder-dominated regime.
key_observables: CDW peak FWHM (delta q_obs), correlation length (xi), integrated intensity (CDW volume), and CDW wavevector (q_CDW).
important_parameters: Temperature (transition at Tc = 27 K), carrier concentration (x=0.115), and the distinction between domain-size effects and intrinsic phase coherence.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a liquid-crystalline framework of stripe order where amplitude and phase fluctuations are decoupled.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) shows a comparison of CDW momentum profiles at Tc (27 K) and lower temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the competition paradigm, describes the experimental setup and the use of aged crystals for disorder studies, presents the RSXS results showing peak narrowing, compares these findings across different cuprate families to demonstrate universality, and concludes with the implications for an intertwined order paradigm.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Momentum profiles of CDW order across supercon- ducting temperature. (a) CDW peak profiles along the h- direction measured at Tc = 27 K (green colored circles) and well below T = 12.4 K (blue circles), together with corre- sponding single Lorentzian fits. The difference in peak width between two temperatures exceeds the instrumental resolu- tion (black line) [43]. (b) Two-dimensional map of the CDW profiles (h vs. T). For each temperature, the profile is nor- malized to its maximum intensity. Inverted triangles mark the FWHM of each peak [44].

FIG. 2. Promotion of CDW phase coherence below Tc. (a) Correlation length of CDW order (grey circles) as a func- tion of temperature, obtained from the peak width using ξ = 2/FWHM. Dashed curves show numerical simulations based on the scattering formalism with (blue) and without (red) a phase change below Tc [44]. The blue squares are sam- pling points generated from the model curve, including points near the experimental temperatures. The arrow denotes the change in CDW phase below Tc. (b) Simulated CDW patterns for an ideally coherent phase (top) with ∼4a periodicity and for a decreased coherent (middle) with finite phase variations, φ. Bottom panel shows the corresponding calculated CDW...

FIG. 3. CDW phase coherence under the disorder. (a) Schematic cartoons of CDW order in the normal state (i.e., T > Tc) and in disordered state, illustrating the charge den- sity topology in CuO2 plane. (b) Cu L3-edge and O K-edge XAS spectra of LSCO (circles) and its relatively disordered LSCO (blue lines). For O XAS spectra, red and gray shaded regions indicate the fitted MCP (or so-called Zhang–Rice sin- glet state) and the upper Hubbard band (UHB), respectively. Dashed line marks the background level. (c) Temperature dependence of the integrated CDW intensity (i.e., CDW vol- ume) for the disordered LSCO (blue circles) compared with the pristine one (grey circles). The arrow highlights...

FIG. 4. CDW-SC interplay through phase-coherence rein- forcement across cuprate families. (a) Temperature depen- dence of the CDW peak-width change ratio, normalized to the width at Tc, for various high-Tc cuprate families mea- sured by RSXS, RIXS, and hard x-ray scattering. Data in- clude this study, LSCO, disordered LSCO, and published data from Bi2212 [17], NBCO [18], Hg1201 [19], YBCO [20] (blue inverted triangles), and LSCO [21] from hard x-ray scattering. The top schematic illustrates an expanded framework for the SC-CDW relationship, where superconductivity can reinforce CDW phase coherence in addition to competing with its vol- ume.
